data.title: Freer Trade Will Help Those Who Need It Most — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Bjorn Lomborg

About the author: Bjorn Lomborg is president of the Copenhagen Consensus, a visiting fellow at Stanford University's Hoover Institution, and author of Best Things First .

As tariffs rock consumer and equity markets around the world, many are starting to wake up to the benefits of specialization and free trade. A majority of Americans believe the U.S. should pursue global free trade. The European Union is striking free trade deals as fast as it can. Even geopolitical rivals China, South Korea, and Japan have agreed to greater cooperation.

The Rust Belt is still solid evidence of the costs of globalization. But freer trade remains overwhelmingly beneficial, both for rich and poor countries, as our peer-reviewed research shows.

Economists conducted the research on behalf of my think tank, the Copenhagen Consensus. It shows that wealthy countries benefit relatively less from free trade, and parts of their workforces carry a disproportionate burden of the costs. Nonetheless, the research found that freer trade is overall beneficial, even for rich countries. Contrary to popular skepticism, a significant part of trade's benefits flow to those who need them most, with poor families needing to spend less on food and clothing.

The researchers' economic model investigated what happens if global trade grows by 5%. To be sure, there would be real and substantial costs. The impacts to the affected workers who lose their jobs, or have to downsize to lower-paid roles, or end up abandoning the workforce altogether, would cost nearly $1 trillion if global trade increased.

Some 92% of that cost would occur in import-exposed markets in high-income countries, such as the Rust Belt in the U.S. These are the places where wages are relatively high, but also where workers are most at-risk from cheaper or better products from poor countries entering rich countries.

These losses are real. But an expansion of free trade would create even more substantial benefits — $6.7 trillion worth among the 38 member countries of the Organization for Economic Cooperation and Development alone. That means that even for rich countries, one dollar of costs delivers $7 of benefits.

In the past, freer trade meant that some factories left industrial centers in developed markets. But free trade simultaneously gave rise to incredible economies of scale that then allowed rich-world consumers to buy cheap and often fairly well-made products from big-box stores. Middle-class Americans currently gain an estimated 29% of their purchasing power from foreign trade. In other words, the average middle-class American can buy nearly one-third more for each dollar compared with if there was no trade. The effect is even bigger — 62% — for the poorest tenth of U.S. consumers. An increase in global trade would amplify this effect.

Perhaps even more important, the research shows that free trade is hugely beneficial for poorer countries. The world's low and lower-middle income countries, which are home to four billion people, will see an estimated $15 billion in costs from slightly increased import competition. But that is dwarfed by the $1.4 trillion in gains they would make through higher export earnings, cheaper imports, and productivity gains from specialization. Trade also attracts foreign investment and creates jobs, pulling millions out of poverty while delivering affordable goods to consumers. And because poorer countries' economies are quite small, the benefits from trade expansion are relatively large. Each dollar in cost would deliver $95 in benefits — an astonishing return on investment.

This is why it is a tragedy that today's political leaders have abandoned the multilateral free trade agenda altogether: Freer trade can generate huge gains for the world's poorest countries. And when the world's poorest countries are better-off, the whole world is a more stable place.

In a world grappling with inequality and economic uncertainty, freer trade remains one of the most powerful tools for shared global prosperity. Its costs — particularly in developed countries — are real and must be addressed with smarter, fairer policies. But the benefits are too significant to ignore. The path forward isn't protectionism, but reform — to make sure the gains of trade aren't only greater, but are also better shared.

Guest commentaries like this one are written by authors outside the Barron's newsroom. They reflect the perspective and opinions of the authors. Submit feedback and commentary pitches to ideas@barrons.com.

data.title: Live Nation case at US Supreme Court tests reach of arbitration law
data.description:
A firm that developed rules for large scale arbitrations has asked the U.S. Supreme Court in a case involving entertainment giant Live Nation to reject a ruling that criticized the procedures as unfair to consumers.

In a friend-of-the-court brief filed on Thursday, dispute resolution firm New Era ADR said the 9th U.S. Circuit Court of Appeals had last year misinterpreted its rules in a decision rejecting Live Nation's efforts to require consumers to agree to arbitrate any disputes when they purchased tickets.

Live Nation was accused of charging artificially high ticket prices in a lawsuit that the event company tried unsuccessfully to move them into arbitration. The 9th Circuit had last year rejected New Era’s arbitration rules and said the consumer plaintiffs could sue Live Nation in a proposed class action in federal court.

New Era is not a party in the lawsuit. It submitted a filing to the justices as a friend of the court, defending its practices. Live Nation has denied any wrongdoing.

The case, which the Supreme Court has not yet agreed to hear, could give the justices a fresh chance to weigh the contours of the Federal Arbitration Act and whether so-called mass arbitration fits into the decades-old law.

Live Nation in its petition to the high court said plaintiffs' lawyers were increasingly pursuing mass arbitration as a pressure tactic to force companies to settle what it called meritless claims.

Live Nation did not immediately respond to a request for comment. A lead attorney for the plaintiffs declined to comment.

New Era chief executive Rich Lee in a statement said the company was "focused on removing gamesmanship and impediments to ensure that all parties have their cases heard and efficiently resolved on their merits."

Companies often promote arbitration as a more efficient way for individual consumers to air their disputes outside of court.

As more companies have employed arbitration agreements to steer consumer claims away from the federal courts, plaintiffs' lawyers have increasingly turned to mass arbitrations, filing thousands of individual arbitration demands that are nearly identical.

Live Nation in 2021 had turned to New Era, a new dispute resolution company, to use its mass arbitration platform.

A federal judge in 2023 had ruled that Live Nation could not enforce its arbitration provisions, and the 9th Circuit had last year upheld the decision.

The 9th Circuit had determined that New Era’s mass arbitration rules were “so dense, convoluted and internally contradictory to be borderline unintelligible.”

New Era’s Supreme Court filing said its rules “have remained centered on keeping mass arbitration workable, accessible, fast, and merits-based for all parties involved.”

In a separate filing on Thursday, lawyers for the consumers urged the justices to leave the 9th Circuit’s order in place.

The case is Live Nation Entertainment et al v. Skot Heckman et al, U.S. Supreme Court, No.

For plaintiffs: Warren Postman of Keller Postman, and Kevin Teruya of Quinn Emanuel

For Live Nation: Roman Martinez and Tim O’Mara of Latham & Watkins

For New Era: Sandra Musumeci of Kelley Drye & Warren

data.title: One of Warren Buffett's Rare Flubs Could Stage a Comeback — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Ian Salisbury

Shares of Occidental Petroleum have been languishing as a result of tepid energy prices and the company's heavy debt load. After Friday's spike in oil prices, the company — known for being one of Warren Buffett's rare flubs — may be worth a second look.

On Friday, the price of futures for Brent crude jumped more than 6.5% to $72.49 a barrel, after Israel attacked numerous targets in Iran, sparking fears of a new conflict in the Middle East. While energy stocks rallied across the board, Occidental was one of the biggest beneficiaries. Shares climbed nearly 3% to $46.

That's got to be a relief to Occidental investors, who have seen the stock slump 23% in the past year amid worries a weak energy outlook would crimp the company's ability to shed its large debt load — about $24 billion at the end of last year.

The massive debt is tied to Occidental's penchant for serial acquisitions. In 2019, the company shelled out more than $50 billion for Anadarko Petroleum, a deal partly funded by issuing $8.5 billion in preferred stock to Buffett's Berkshire Hathaway.

The company had only partly worked through its Anadarko debt when it spent another $12 billion to acquire private energy company CrownRock Resources last August. The deal led to a wave of analyst downgrades. Confidence flagged further when Occidental posted mixed fourth-quarter results in February, partly blaming weak oil prices. At the time oil was trading at just under $70 a barrel.

Despite the company's woes, bulls point out Occidental has made steady progress paying down its debt. In May note, Raymond James analyst John Freeman noted that the company has reduced its debt by $2.4 billion year to date, and by $6.8 billion since the third quarter of 2024.

While Occidental does pay a dividend — the stock yields about 2.1% — investors should look for additional balance sheet improvements. "In 2025, the company is prioritizing debt reduction and cash flow growth through reinvestment over share buybacks and preferred equity redemptions," wrote Freeman, who rates the stock "Outperform," with a $51 price target.

The current debt target is around $15 billion, which it seems likely to hit in 2027. With analysts expecting about $4 billion in annual free cash flow over the next two years that should be doable. Of course, every dollar that oil prices climb make the goal a little bit easier.

Write to Ian Salisbury at ian.salisbury@barrons.com

data.title: Sherwin-Williams Shares Fall After Citigroup Downgrade
data.description:
Sherwin-Williams shares were down about 5% in recent Friday trading after Citigroup downgraded the company's stock to neutral from buy and cut its price target to $385 from $405.

The company's trading volume for the day stood at about 1.6 million shares against a daily average of roughly 1.7 million shares.

data.title: Cargo Traffic to Los Angeles Port Expected to End on High Note in June After US-China Trade Deal
data.description:
Cargo traffic at the Port of Los Angeles is expected to end on a high note in June after the US and China reached a trade deal subject to their leaders' approval.

A total of 123,878 20-foot equivalent units, a standardized measure of cargo capacity, will likely reach the port in the week ending June 28, according to data from ship tracking system Port Optimizer. That volume reflects a nearly 1.5% rise on a weekly basis and a 55% increase annually.

Twenty three freight vessels are scheduled to arrive at the port in the week ending June 28, Port Optimizer data showed. That's up from 17 vessels this week and from 21 in the week ending June 21.

Earlier this week, US President Donald Trump said a trade deal with China is "done," subject to his and Chinese President Xi Jinping's approval. However, any positive impact from the announcement was blunted by Trump's warning later that he would impose tariffs on countries that failed to agree to new trade deals.

Trump previously accused China of breaching a preliminary trade agreement brokered by US Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent that suspended most levies on each other's goods for 90 days.

data.title: CIBC's Week Ahead Market Call For Canada
data.description:
CIBC duo Ali Jaffery and Katherine Judge said political developments will likely garner more attention than the data calendar, with the G7 summit taking place early in the week.

On the data, they noted the advance estimate for retail sales in April, with final figures due out next Friday, implied a solid start to the quarter for consumption, as consumers apparently extended tariff front-running efforts. But CIBC expects a deterioration in the May advance estimate as that effect fades and a weak labour market weighs.

Homebuilding, CIBC added, likely cooled following a surge in the prior month, and in line with subdued home sales. The bank noted data for both May Housing Starts and Existing Sales is due out Monday.

(Also on the calendar for next week is April International Securities Transactions data Tuesday. The Bank of Canada's Summary of Deliberations, a summary of monetary policy deliberations by the Governing Council for the policy decision that was announced two weeks earlier, is also due out that day. The following day BoC Governor Tiff Macklem delivers a speech to the St. John's Board of Trade.

Also, May Industrial Product Prices and Raw Materials data for May is slated for release Friday.)

data.title: Center Files for 3.75 Million Share IPO, Expected Range of $4 to $6 per Share
data.description:
Center Holdings filed on Friday a registration statement for an initial public offering of nearly 3.8 million shares, with an expected price range of $4 to $6 per share.

The company said it has applied to list its shares on the Nasdaq Capital Market under the "CTMB" ticker though listing approval is still pending.

Center said that if it prices its IPO at $4 per share, the low end of its expected range, the net proceeds are expected to be $12.8 million. If underwriters exercise their full overallotment option of up to 15% of shares, net proceeds could rise to $14.8 million, the company said.

About 40% of the proceeds will be used to develop and expand Center's water dispenser business, and another 40% to grow its original smartphone business. The rest will go toward expanding its existing operations, the company said.

In addition to the IPO, Center said it is registering up to 3.97 million shares for resale by existing shareholders through a separate resale prospectus. These resale shares can only be sold after the IPO is successfully completed. The company said it will not receive any proceeds from the sale of those resale shares.

data.title: There are risks that the U.S. economy will suffer collateral damage from Israel's airstrikes in Iran
data.description:
By Greg Robb

Reliance on overseas oil is very much less a factor today than 10 or 15 years ago, says Nomura economist

The airstrikes launched by Israel inside Iran will only have a small impact on the U.S. economy, said David Seif, chief economist for developed markets at Nomura.

"I think the risk to the U.S. economy is relatively small," Seif said, in an interview with MarketWatch.

"Recession risks are higher, but only by a tiny bit," he added.

A steady increase in oil prices - due to the risk that an escalation in Mideast tensions curtails oil production - is the biggest threat to the U.S. economy posed by the Israel-Iran clash. Looking at the geopolitical chessboard, this seems unlikely, Seif said.

Oil (CL00) (WBS00) (BRN00) has risen as a natural reaction to the conflict, but Saudi Arabia has shown a capacity to increase its output, Seif said.

Jim Baird, chief investment officer with Plante Moran Financial Advisors, agreed, saying that "a sustained increase in oil prices doesn't look like the base-case scenario."

If the conflict is largely contained to Israel and Iran, it's even possible that oil prices could recede, Baird added, in a note to clients.

'There is essentially no chance of the Fed cutting interest rates next week, and I don't think this changes that at all.'David Seif, Nomura

Stepping back, the U.S. economy's reliance on overseas oil is very much less a factor today than 10 or 15 years ago, Seif said.

Drivers may pay higher prices at the gas pump, but overall this isn't a clear negative to the macroeconomy. The picture is different in Japan and Europe, which do rely on imported oil, he said.

Energy prices may boost inflation in coming months, but the Federal Reserve will focus on core prices, which exclude volatile food and energy prices, he said.

That means no near-term impact on monetary policy.

"There is essentially no chance of the Fed cutting interest rates next week, and I don't think this changes that at all," Seif said.

The well-known University of Pennsylvania economist Jeremy Siegel said the market reaction to Israel strike had been tellingly mild and would have been much worse a decade ago. "Iran is much weaker. What can it do?" Siegel said, in an interview on CNBC.

Tehran has vowed to respond harshly to the Israeli strikes, which continued on Friday.

-Greg Robb

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

McDonald's Reaches Settlement Over Allegations of Racially Discriminatory Ad Spending
data.description:
By Kelly Cloonan

McDonald's said it has reached a settlement with two companies owned by media mogul Byron Allen that accused the fast-food giant of discriminating against Black-owned media companies.

The fast-food restaurant company said Friday it would continue to purchase advertising from Entertainment Studios Network in a manner that aligns with its advertising strategy, and the network would dismiss its lawsuit against McDonald's.

Under the confidential terms of the agreement, McDonald's isn't admitting any wrongdoing, and the ads sold would be priced at market value, the company said.

Entertainment Studios Network and The Weather Group had filed a complaint against McDonald's in 2021, seeking $10 billion in damages as they alleged the restaurant chain engaged in a "discriminatory contracting process and refusals to advertise on Plaintiffs' networks on the basis of race."

Entertainment Studios Network and The Weather Group said they are pleased with the resolution of the lawsuit.

"During the course of this litigation, many of our preconceptions have been clarified, and we acknowledge McDonald's commitment to investing in Black-owned media properties and increasing access to opportunity," the companies said.

McDonald's said it is pleased Allen has come to appreciate the company's commitment to inclusion, and that the commercial arrangement is consistent with its other supplier relationships.

data.title: Vantage Corp Announces Closing of $13 Million Initial Public Offering
data.description:
SINGAPORE, June 13, 2025 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) — Vantage Corp (NYSE American: VNTG) (“Vantage” or the “Company”), a shipbroking company providing comprehensive services including brokerage, consultancy, and operational support in the tanker market, today announced the closing of its underwritten initial public offering (the “Offering”) of 3,250,000 Class A Ordinary Shares at a public offering price of $4.00 per share, for aggregate gross proceeds of approximately $13 million, prior to deducting underwriting discounts and other offering expenses. The Offering closed on June 13, 2025 and the Company’s Class A Ordinary Shares began trading on the NYSE American on June 12, 2025, under the symbol “VNTG”.

In addition, the Company has granted the underwriters a 45-day option to purchase up to an additional 487,500 Common Shares at the public offering price per share to cover over-allotments, if any.

Network 1 Financial Securities, Inc., a full-service broker/dealer, acted as the sole managing underwriter and bookrunner for the initial public offering. Loeb & Loeb LLP acted as legal counsel to Vantage Corp and Hunter Taubman Fischer & Li LLC acted as legal counsel to Network 1 Financial Securities, Inc. for the Offering.

The Offering is being conducted pursuant to the Company’s registration statement on Form F-1, as amended (File No. 333-282566), relating to the shares being sold in the Offering initially filed with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (the “SEC”) on October 9, 2024, and was declared effective by the SEC on June 11, 2025. The Offering was made only by means of a prospectus. A final prospectus relating to the Offering has been filed with the SEC on June 13, 2025, and is available on the SEC’s website at www.sec.gov. Copies of the final prospectus related to the Offering may be obtained from Network 1 Financial Securities, Inc., The Galleria, 2 Bridge Avenue, Suite 241, Red Bank, NJ 07701.

data.title: Week's Best: Schwab Is Cutting Fees — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Kenneth Corbin

Charles Schwab will reduce the fees it charges on four index exchange-traded funds and split shares on six of its mutual funds, which will lower the price per share. The fee cuts are modest — between two and four basis points — but Schwab says they will bring the cost of all its equity and fixed-income market cap-weighted index ETFs to less than 10 basis points. The cuts continue a trend of fee reductions in the industry, and follow similar recent moves by Vanguard and E*Trade.

In other most-read wealth management articles this week:

What is Sammons' wealth strategy ? Long known as a prominent player in insurance and annuities, Sammons Financial Group is making a strategic push into wealth management. The firm, based in West Des Moines, Iowa, recently announced the acquisition of the $8 billion advisory business Wealthcare, which caters to a mass-affluent client base. That followed earlier acquisitions of a firm that specializes in model portfolios for retirees and an advisory practice specializing in family-office services for high-net-worth clients.

Brokerage firms settle charges of excessive commissions . Edward Jones, LPL Financial, and three other major brokerage firms have agreed to pay $9.3 million in penalties to resolve charges brought by state regulators that they overcharged clients for commissions on small-dollar stock trades. Those firms, along with RBC, Stifel, and TD Ameritrade, agreed to establish safeguards to ensure they don't revert to charging excessive fees. Regulators allege that the firms routinely charged their minimum commission for small-dollar trades, often exceeding the cap for a commission as a percentage of the transaction.

Morgan Stanley says it's "underweight" in alts . Morgan Stanley prides itself on being the largest distributor of alternative investments, and CEO Ted Pick says at times its activity in the field has eclipsed all competitors combined. But in its fast-growing wealth management channel, exposure to alts has lagged, Pick said at a conference this week. Morgan Stanley's advisors allocate roughly 5% of clients' funds to alternative investments such as private credit and private equity, but Pick suggests that a better proportion would be closer to 10% or 15%.

CFP chair on the changing planning profession . A lot has changed since Liz Miller became a certified financial planner in 2008, a time that seems recent enough but was almost impossibly analog compared with today's world. Miller, the chair of the CFP Board, sat down with Barron's Advisor to take stock of the profession, sharing her views on how artificial intelligence is reshaping the field, how aspiring planners stumble and sometimes flame out, and what types of careers in nonfinancial industries produce some of the best financial planners.

New Commonwealth recruit discusses LPL transition . The same day Ralph Angelo joined Commonwealth Financial Network, the firm announced it was selling itself to LPL Financial. Angelo, who came to Commonwealth from Osaic, had hoped that would be the last time he would switch firms. Angelo says he took a "good long look" at the new arrangement and concluded that if his 30-person, $1.5 billion team was going to have to find another home, LPL "would make the most sense." In an interview with Barron's Advisor, Angelo discusses the potential to scale his practice as part of LPL.

data.title: The Israel-Iran conflict is casting a long shadow on the stock market. Here are the winners and losers.
data.description:
By James Rogers and Tomi Kilgore

The Middle East conflict is impacting a wide range of sectors besides the obvious oil and defense names, as a surge in oil prices can affect consumer behavior in many ways

The Israel-Iran conflict is casting a long shadow on Wall Street, as the stocks of companies in multiple sectors - besides just the obvious oil and defense sectors - were among the day's biggest movers.

Among key factors, a big jump in gasoline prices as a result of the conflict could have an effect on consumer and investor behavior in a number of ways.

But first, the obvious: Defense contractor RTX Corp.'s stock (RTX) was up 3.2% in afternoon trading, while shares of rivals Lockheed Martin Corp. (LMT) gained 3.6% and those of L3HarrisTechnologies Inc. (LHX) rose 2.6%.

Read: These defense stocks offer the best growth prospects, as the Israel-Iran conflict fuels new interest in the sector.

And with crude-oil futures (CL00) (CL.1) (BRN00) rising more than 5% toward a four-month high, oil giant Exxon Mobil Corp.'s stock (XOM) was up 1.3% and headed for a sixth straight gain, while ConocoPhillips shares (COP) rose 1.2% and the Energy Select Sector SPDR ETF XLE was up 1.2%. Chevron Corp.'s stock (CVX) was a more modest gainer, rising 0.2%.

In comparison, the S&P 500 index SPX was down 0.7%.

And speaking of higher oil and gasoline prices, shares of carmaker General Motors Co. (GM) were down 0.9% and rival Ford Motor Co. shares (F) gave up 0.6%.

Meanwhile, shares of companies that allow consumers to bypass the use of oil and gasoline were getting a lift. Electric-vehicle maker Tesla Inc.'s stock (TSLA) jumped 4.1%, while shares of solar-power companies First Solar Inc. (FSLR) climbed 2.6% and those of Israel-based SolarEdge Technologies Inc. (SEDG) advanced 3.5%.

Rising oil prices, of course, can also impact consumer spending by usurping wallet share. Target Corp.'s stock (TGT) slid 2.6%, while Best Buy Co. Inc. (BBY) shares slid 2.5% and Deckers Outdoor Corp.'s stock (DECK) was down 1.8%. Nike Inc.'s stock (NKE) fell 1.1%.

Shares of Walmart Inc. (WMT) were down just 0.3% but were headed for an eighth straight loss, which would be their longest losing streak in 11 years.

See related: Walmart's stock looks like it's in trouble. What the chart says may come next.

Inflation is also a key factor in consumer spending. In a note released Friday, Raymond James Chief Economist Eugenio Aleman also weighed the potential impact of the Israel-Iran conflict on inflation. "The truth is that one of the most important contributors to lower inflation over the last year or so has been a decline in petroleum prices, and the current conflict will probably disrupt this trend," he wrote.

Airline stocks were also taking a hit, as higher fuel costs were seen as leading to higher ticket prices, which would further crimp travel demand. American Airlines Group Inc. shares (AAL) were down 2.6%, Delta Air Lines Inc.'s stock (DAL) was declining 1.7% and United Airlines Holdings Inc.'s stock (UAL) was down 2.2%. The U.S. Global Jets ETF JETS declined 1.9%.

Worries about travel also impacted cruise-operator stocks, as Carnival Corp.'s shares (CCL) declined 3.7%, Norwegian Cruise Line Holdings Ltd.'s stock (NCLH) was down 2.6%, and Royal Caribbean Group's shares (RCL) fell 1.7%.

If flying is affected, then companies that provide postflight services, such as hotel operators and car-rental companies, will also feel some pain.

Hilton Worldwide Holdings Inc. (HLT) saw its stock fall 1.9%, while Marriott International Inc. shares (MAR) declined 2.2% and Airbnb Inc.'s stock (ABNB) gave up 2.2%. And Avis Budget Group Inc.'s stock (CAR) was down 2.2% and Hertz Global Holdings Inc. shares (HTZ) lost 2.4%.

Let's not forget U.S.-listed shares of some well-known companies based in Israel.

Check Point Software Technologies Ltd.'s stock (CHKP) shed 1.1%, Teva Pharmaceutical Industries Ltd. shares (TEVA) slid 2.7% and shares of autonomous-driving technology company Mobileye Global Inc. (MBLY) dropped 1.8%.

And shares of eToro Group Ltd. (ETOR), the Tel Aviv-based stock- and crypto-trading platform that went public to much fanfare last month, were losing 3.4%.

-James Rogers -Tomi Kilgore
